standard_description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Task
Materials
ruler, meter sticks, yard sticks, measuring tape
paper
crayons
Actions
Part 1
Explain that students will be working in pairs to determine the length of each partner’s foot. Ask what tools would be appropriate for determining length.  Chart student thinking about appropriate tools, and today’s goal of  using standard measurement tools such as rulers, yard sticks, meter sticks, or measuring tape.
Ask student pairs to consider the unit(s) that would give the most precise measure of the length of a student’s foot.  If students struggle with this idea, the teacher might model using his/her foot as an example considering yards, meters, feet, inches and centimeters.
Ask student pairs to identify the unit they will work with and predict the length of each partner’s foot and record their estimations.  Estimates should be recorded in units.
Part 2
Have one student from each pair stand on a blank sheet of paper and have his/her partner make a mark at the student’s heel and another at the toe.
Then, together, have the pair measure the distance between the two marks to determine the length of the first student’s foot and record the length.  Students may have to find the closest unit.
Have students then find the difference between the estimation and actual length.
Repeat with the second student, using a different color to make the marks.  The second student should stand at the same endpoint as the first student so that students can visually compare the two representations and compare the number of the distance in units in part three.
Part 3
Working in pairs, have students compare the distances between the lengths of each student’s foot.
Class Discussion
Engaging in a class discussion will support students in thinking about the mathematical ideas embedded within the task.
What tool did you use and why?
How was that tool helpful?
If you were going to measure _____ (a desk, a whiteboard, an eraser), would you use the same tool? Why?
How long was your foot? What was your estimate? What was the difference?
What unit did you use to measure? How did you decide to use ______? Why is the unit important?
How long was your partner’s foot? What was his/her estimate? What was the difference?
What was the difference between the length of your foot and the length of your partner’s foot? How did you find the difference?
What did you notice about finding the difference? Is there more than one way to find the difference? (This might be an opportunity to discuss finding the difference by measuring how much longer one length is than the other or by using an operation such as addition to add up from the smallest length to the larger length or by subtracting the smaller length from the larger length.)

Problem 1
Fill in the blank to make each equation true.
a.   4 feet = ______ inches
b.   3 yards 1 foot = ______ feet
Problem 2
Determine whether each statement below is true or false. If the statement is false, change the right side of the comparison to make it true.
a.   6 yards < 20 feet __________________
b.   7 feet 1 inch > 90 inches __________________

Problem 1
Use a yardstick and a 12-inch ruler to answer the following questions.
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a yard and the length of a foot?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a foot and the length of an inch?
Problem 2
Fill in the following conversion tables.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Janella wants to go on an amusement park ride. She sees this sign before she gets in line for it:
###IMAGE1###
Janella knows that she’s 1 yard 8 inches tall. Is she tall enough to go on the amusement ride?
Problem 4
Show students
this image
from a CNN article with the information below.
###TABLE0###
How much taller is the tallest man than the shortest man?

paper
crayons

Ask student pairs to identify the unit they will work with and predict the length of each partner’s foot and record their estimations.  Estimates should be recorded in units.
Part 2

Then, together, have the pair measure the distance between the two marks to determine the length of the first student’s foot and record the length.  Students may have to find the closest unit.
Have students then find the difference between the estimation and actual length.
Repeat with the second student, using a different color to make the marks.  The second student should stand at the same endpoint as the first student so that students can visually compare the two representations and compare the number of the distance in units in part three.
Part 3

Class Discussion

What tool did you use and why?
How was that tool helpful?
If you were going to measure _____ (a desk, a whiteboard, an eraser), would you use the same tool? Why?
How long was your foot? What was your estimate? What was the difference?
What unit did you use to measure? How did you decide to use ______? Why is the unit important?
How long was your partner’s foot? What was his/her estimate? What was the difference?
What was the difference between the length of your foot and the length of your partner’s foot? How did you find the difference?
What did you notice about finding the difference? Is there more than one way to find the difference? (This might be an opportunity to discuss finding the difference by measuring how much longer one length is than the other or by using an operation such as addition to add up from the smallest length to the larger length or by subtracting the smaller length from the larger length.)

Problem 1
Complete the conversion table.
Distance
###TABLE0###
Problem 2
Fill in the blank to make the equation true.
21 km 80 m = ___________ m
Problem 3
The female world record for high jump is 2 meters and 9 centimeters. How high is that in centimeters?

Task
Materials
ruler, meter sticks, yard sticks, measuring tape
paper
crayons
Actions
Part 1
Explain that students will be working in pairs to determine the length of each partner’s foot. Ask what tools would be appropriate for determining length.  Chart student thinking about appropriate tools, and today’s goal of  using standard measurement tools such as rulers, yard sticks, meter sticks, or measuring tape.

Class Discussion

What tool did you use and why?
How was that tool helpful?
If you were going to measure _____ (a desk, a whiteboard, an eraser), would you use the same tool? Why?
How long was your foot? What was your estimate? What was the difference?
What unit did you use to measure? How did you decide to use ______? Why is the unit important?
How long was your partner’s foot? What was his/her estimate? What was the difference?
What was the difference between the length of your foot and the length of your partner’s foot? How did you find the difference?
What did you notice about finding the difference? Is there more than one way to find the difference? (This might be an opportunity to discuss finding the difference by measuring how much longer one length is than the other or by using an operation such as addition to add up from the smallest length to the larger length or by subtracting the smaller length from the larger length.)

Problem 1
Use a meter stick to answer the following questions.
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a millimeter and the length of a centimeter?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a centimeter and the length of a meter?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a millimeter and the length of a meter?
Problem 2
Fill in the following conversion tables.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Mr. Duffy wants to buy a new pair of skis. Skis are sold based on their length in centimeters. If Mr. Duffy wants to buy a pair of skis that are his exact height, and he is 1 meter 83 centimeters tall, what length skis should Mr. Duffy buy?
Problem 4
Two sisters stand back to back to see how much taller the older sister is than the younger sister.
###IMAGE1###
The older sister is 1 m 30 cm tall. The younger sister is 24 cm 2 mm shorter than her. How tall is the younger sister?

khan_exercises.title: Estimate lengths
khan_exercises.description: Estimate lengths using units of centimeters and meters.

items.question_content:
Penny is $25$ centimeters tall.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/89fe48d349774409e48c733ed2efd12197e21f1b)

**About how tall is Penny's dad?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Penny's dad is a **little taller** than Penny.
Is $65$ centimeters, $15$ centimeters or $35$ centimeters a **little taller** than $25$ centimeters?
Penny's dad is about $35$ centimeters tall.

items.question_content:
The first sunflower is $40$ centimeters tall.  

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64db762fe4e70f4febe563a00d2470458503a034)

**About how tall is the second sunflower?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The second sunflower is **much shorter** than the first sunflower.
Is $50$ centimeters, $40$ centimeters or $20$ centimeters **much shorter** than $40$ centimeters?
The second sunflower is about $20$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
The chicken egg is $6$ centimeters tall.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/33c24be2c1c5eaf966a1fa871506b8c94d6723d9)

**About how tall is the ostrich egg?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The ostrich egg is **about double** the height of the chicken egg.
Is $12$ centimeters, $24$ centimeters or $4$ centimeters **about double** than $6$ centimeters?
The ostrich egg is about $12$ centimeters tall.

items.question_content:
Porky Pig's slide is $8$ meters long.  

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6a1724e775b5623abb3fd1f4b99875a47891f834)

**About how long is Danny Dog's slide?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Danny's slide is **slightly shorter** than Porky's slide.
Is $5$ meters, $2$ meters or $10$ meters **slightly shorter** than $8$ meters?
Danny Dog's slide is about $5$ meters.

items.question_content:
Sammy Shark is $50$ meters from the beach.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5d1dfc75a10f5231a465f69a2dbebd98f9a9aaaa)

**About how far is Tammy Turtle from the beach?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Tammy Turtle is **much closer** to the beach than Sammy Shark.
Is $15$ meters, $45$ meters or $75$ meters **much closer** to the beach than $50$ meters?
Tammy Turtle is about $15$ meters from the beach.

items.question_content:
The fox rolled his basketball $10$ meters.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd6de9a39c5d0226ce8ce4c16f0b7878a20e4fb9)

**About how far did the pig roll her basketball?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The pig rolled her basketball **much farther** than the fox.
Is $12$ meters, $2$ meters or $22$ meters **much farther** than $10$ meters?
The pig rolled her basketball about $22$ meters.

khan_exercises.title: Estimate lengths (US Customary units)
khan_exercises.description: Estimate lengths using units of inches and feet.

items.question_content:
Fiona Fox's slide is $8$ feet long.  

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e4427690173d0df385a3435335d4188588afb361)

**About how long is Sammy Squirrel's slide?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sammy's slide is **slightly shorter** than Fiona's slide.
Is $5$ feet, $2$ feet or $10$ feet **slightly shorter** than $8$ feet?
Sammy Squirrel's slide is about $5$ feet.

items.question_content:
Tammy Turtle is $50$ feet from the beach.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b4525cf1805a4c206ea36286cb18433bd4e9cc7f)

**About how far is Wally Whale from the beach?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Wally Whale is **a little farther** away from the beach than Tammy Turtle.
Is $25$ feet, $150$ feet or $500$ feet **a little farther** than $50$ feet?
Wally Whale is about $150$ feet from the beach.

items.question_content:
The horse has a length of $9$ feet.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2cb8d073ac0dc32d7dadd7ceac35e95ee6c5569f)

**About how long is the cow?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The cow's length is **barely shorter** than the horse.
Is $20$ feet, $8$ feet or $3$ feet **barely shorter** than $9$ feet?
The cow has a length of about $8$ feet.

items.question_content:
The hamburger has a length of $5$ inches.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/36af92e9af756ba0223af7519282432a0d539f8e)

**About how long is the hot dog?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The hot dog is a **little longer** than the hamburger.
Is $3$ inches, $9$ inches or $18$ inches **slightly longer** than $5$ inches?
The hot dog has a length of about $9$ inches.

items.question_content:
Penny is $25$ inches tall.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e261edb6e1070af2d6a1e6e8de0a6dd163d397e2)

**About how tall is Penny's dad?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Penny's dad is a **little taller** than Penny.
Is $15$ inches, $35$ inches or $65$ inches a **little taller** than $25$ inches?
Penny's dad is about $35$ inches tall.

items.question_content:
The first sunflower is $32$ inches tall.  

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2dde684bb235869d254264cac65ac739d9ac80d7) 

**About how tall is the second sunflower?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The second sunflower is **much shorter** than the first sunflower.
Is $30$ inches, $18$ inches or $40$ inches a **much shorter** than $32$ inches?
The second sunflower is about $18$ inches.
